content.full_text:
1100 K STREET BUILDING, 
December 9, 1976 
Mr. H. P. (Hal) Shawlee 
Manager, Civic Affairs 
Union Oil Company of California 
Union Oil Center 
P. O. Box 7600 
Los Angeles, California 
90051 
Dear Hal, 
95814 
Thank you for your letter of November 18 expressing concern 
about the apparent closeness of filing deadlines for campaign 
reports required by the Political Reform Act. 
The law, however, does not require a committee or candidate 
filing in connection with an election during the previous six 
month period to file an off-year report in addition to filing 
campaign statements in connection with an election. 
(Government 
Code Section 84206) 
After filing a post-election campaign statement in connection 
with the November 2, 1976 general election the committee will not be 
required to file a campaign statement again, unless contributions 
or expenditures are made in connection with a local or special 
election, until July 31, covering the period from December 31, 1976 
through June 30, 1977. 
If you have any additional questions concerning this matter 
please let me know. 
DHL:av 
truly yours, 
H. LOWENSTEIN 
Chairman 


unl 
Union Oil Compan 
ifornia 
Union Oil Center, P.O. Box 7600, Los Angeles, California 9005' 
Telephone (213) 486-6446 
H. P. 
Shawlee 
November 18, 1976 
Manager, Civic Affairs 
Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
California Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear Dan: 
The schedule for filing reports in connection with the November 2 
General Election calls for the post-election report to be submitted 
on January 6, 1977 with a closing date of December 30, 1976. 
The general regulations call for a report by January 31, 1977 with a 
closing date of December 31, 1976 -- just one day after the closing 
date for the post-election report. 
In the interest of reasonableness, it would seem that those two 
reports could be combined into one -- with a closing date of 
December 31,1976. Full disclosure would be accomplished but 
unnecessary paperwork would be eliminated. 
Would your commission consider adopting a regulation which would 
permit those two reports to be combined. 
Sincerely, 
HPS/dkv

sections.question: The schedule for filing reports in connection with the November 2 General Election calls for the post-election report to be submitted on January 6, 1977 with a closing date of December 30, 1976. The general regulations call for a report by January 31, 1977 with a closing date of December 31, 1976 -- just one day after the closing date for the post-election report. In the interest of reasonableness, it would seem that those two reports could be combined into one -- with a closing date of December 31, 1976. Full disclosure would be accomplished but unnecessary paperwork would be eliminated. Would your commission consider adopting a regulation which would permit those two reports to be combined.
sections.conclusion: The law, however, does not require a committee or candidate filing in connection with an election during the previous six month period to file an off-year report in addition to filing campaign statements in connection with an election. (Government Code Section 84206) After filing a post-election campaign statement in connection with the November 2, 1976 general election the committee will not be required to file a campaign statement again, unless contributions or expenditures are made in connection with a local or special election, until July 31, covering the period from December 31, 1976 through June 30, 1977.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether campaign committees can combine the post-election report (due January 6 with December 30 closing date) with the general off-year report (due January 31 with December 31 closing date) to reduce redundant filings while maintaining full disclosure.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under Government Code Section 84206, committees are not required to file an off-year report in addition to election-related campaign statements. After filing the post-election statement for the November 2, 1976 election, no additional filing is required until July 31, 1977 (covering December 31, 1976 through June 30, 1977), unless activity occurs in local or special elections.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (November 18, 1976) and the FPPC response letter (December 9, 1976). Both question and conclusion sections are verbatim from the respective letters. Clear statutory reference provided (Government Code Section 84206).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The schedule for filing reports in connection with the November 2 General Election calls for the post-election report to be submitted on January 6, 1977 with a closing date of December 30, 1976. The general regulations call for a report by January 31, 1977 with a closing date of December 31, 1976 -- just one day after the closing date for the post-election report. In the interest of reasonableness, it would seem that those two reports could be combined into one -- with a closing date of December 31, 1976. Full disclosure would be accomplished but unnecessary paperwork would be eliminated. Would your commission consider adopting a regulation which would permit those two reports to be combined.

CONCLUSION: The law, however, does not require a committee or candidate filing in connection with an election during the previous six month period to file an off-year report in addition to filing campaign statements in connection with an election. (Government Code Section 84206) After filing a post-election campaign statement in connection with the November 2, 1976 general election the committee will not be required to file a campaign statement again, unless contributions or expenditures are made in connection with a local or special election, until July 31, covering the period from December 31, 1976 through June 30, 1977.

embedding.summary: FPPC Chairman Lowenstein responds to Union Oil Company's request to combine the post-election campaign report with the off-year general report to reduce filing burdens. The FPPC clarifies that California law does not require an off-year report in addition to election-related campaign statements, resolving the perceived conflict in filing deadlines.
